This project demonstrates how data science is used in healthcare to analyze medical insurance data and understand the factors that influence healthcare costs.The analysis focuses on identifying relationships between demographic characteristics, lifestyle habits, and insurance charges to generate meaningful insights for healthcare decision-making and predictive modeling.

Project Objectives

  • Data Preparation: Clean and preprocess healthcare insurance data for analysis
  • Pattern Exploration: Explore patterns and trends in medical insurance charges
  • Demographic Analysis: Investigate how demographic factors such as age and gender influence healthcare expenses
  • Lifestyle Impact: Examine the impact of lifestyle factors such as: Smoking habits, Body Mass Index (BMI)on insurance costs
  • Family Variables: Analyze family-related variables such as the number of dependents covered by insurance
  • Geographic Comparison: Compare insurance charges across different geographic regions

Data Collection and Loading¶

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np 
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

plt.figure(figsize = (5, 5))
sns.boxplot(data = df, y = "bmi")
plt.title("Boxplot of bmi distribution")
plt.ylabel("bmi")
plt.show()
In [29]:
Q1 = df["bmi"].quantile(0.25)  
Q3 = df["bmi"].quantile(0.75)
IQR = Q3 - Q1  

lower_bound = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR 
upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR  
df1 = df[(df["bmi"] >= lower_bound) & (df["bmi"] <= upper_bound)]
print("outliers removed successfully")

outliers removed successfully

plt.figure(figsize = (5, 5))
sns.boxplot(data = df1, y = "bmi")
plt.title("Boxplot of bmi distribution")
plt.ylabel("bmi")
plt.show()

Data Visualization¶

Frequency of insurance charges¶

In [47]:
import seaborn as sns

sns.histplot(df7, x="charges", bins=10, kde=True, color="blue")
plt.xlabel("Charges in ksh")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.title("Histogram of Charges Distribution")
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

Key Insights¶

The graph is "right-skewed" (or positively skewed). This means most of the data is bunched up on the left side (lower costs), while a long "tail" stretches out toward the higher costs on the right. The highest frequency is in the very first bin, meaning the most common charges are in the $1,000 to $5,000 range. As the cost increases, the frequency generally drops. There’s a significant "cliff" or drop-off after $15,000, where the number of occurrences becomes much smaller.

In [48]:
import seaborn as sns

sns.histplot(df7, x="age", bins=10, kde=True, color="blue")
plt.xlabel("age")
plt.ylabel("frequency")
plt.title("Histogram of age distribution")
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
In [49]:
import seaborn as sns

sns.histplot(df7, x="bmi", bins=10, kde=True, color="blue")
plt.xlabel("bmi")
plt.ylabel("frequency")
plt.title("Histogram of age distribution")
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

Key Insights¶

The histogram shows that the BMI (labeled as “bmi” on the x-axis, though the title mentions age distribution) follows an approximately bell-shaped, near-normal distribution centered around 28–31, where the highest frequency of observations occurs. Most individuals fall within the BMI range of about 24 to 35, indicating that the dataset is concentrated around moderate-to-high BMI values. The smooth blue density curve confirms this central clustering and suggests a fairly symmetric pattern, although there is a slight right skew, as values extend further toward 45 than toward the lower end near 16. Extremely low and extremely high BMI values are relatively rare, shown by the much smaller bars at both tails. Overall, the distribution indicates a population with predominantly overweight BMI levels, moderate variability, and no major outliers or multiple peaks, implying a single dominant group rather than separate subpopulations.

Scatterplot for charges vs age by genders¶

In [69]:
plt.figure()
sns.scatterplot(df7, x="age", y="charges", hue="sex", size="charges", alpha=0.9, palette=['red', 'blue'])
sns.regplot(x='age', y='charges', data=df7, scatter_kws={'alpha':0.5}, line_kws={"color":"red"})
plt.title("Charges vs Age by Gender")
plt.xlabel('Age')
plt.ylabel('Charges')
plt.legend(title='gender')
plt.show()

Key Insight:¶

The graph shows that medical charges generally increase with age, but the population is clearly divided into two distinct groups. Most people follow a predictable, lower-cost path that climbs steadily as they get older, while a smaller, separate group faces much higher charges regardless of their age. Because gender is evenly mixed in both groups, it isn't the cause of this price gap; the separation suggests that other significant factors—like lifestyle or health conditions—are what actually push certain individuals into that high-cost bracket.

Scatter plot for charges vs age by smoker¶

In [67]:
plt.figure()
sns.scatterplot(df7, x="age", y="charges", hue="smoker", size="charges", alpha=0.9, palette=['red', 'blue'])
plt.title("Charges vs Age by Smoker")
plt.xlabel('Age')
plt.ylabel('Charges')
plt.legend(title='gender')
plt.show()

Key Insight:¶

This graph confirms that smoking is a massive driver of costs, regardless of age. While both groups show charges increasing as people get older, smokers (top clusters) consistently pay thousands of dollars more than non-smokers (bottom clusters) at every age level. Essentially, smoking effectively "shifts" an individual into a much higher payment bracket, where even a young smoker might pay more than a non-smoker who is decades older.

Scatterplot for charges vs bmi by smoker¶

In [65]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt 
plt.figure()
sns.scatterplot(df7, x="bmi", y="charges", hue="smoker", size="charges", alpha=0.9, palette=['red', 'blue'])
plt.title("charges vs Body Mass Index by smoker")
plt.xlabel('bmi')
plt.ylabel('Charges')
plt.legend(title='gender')
plt.show()

Key Insights¶

This graph shows that smoking status is the biggest factor in determining high costs, far outweighing BMI. While non-smokers (blue) stay in a lower price bracket regardless of their weight, smokers (red) are almost all pushed into the most expensive tier. Essentially, if you smoke, your charges are likely to be very high even with a lower BMI, whereas non-smokers generally maintain much lower costs even as their BMI increases.

Boxplot¶

In [53]:
df7.head()
Out[53]:
age sex bmi children smoker region charges
0 19 female 27.900 0 yes southwest 16884.92400
1 18 male 33.770 1 no southeast 1725.55230
2 28 male 33.000 3 no southeast 4449.46200
3 33 male 22.705 0 no northwest 21984.47061
4 32 male 28.880 0 no northwest 3866.85520
In [72]:
sns.boxplot(x='smoker', y='charges', palette="Set1", data=df7)
plt.xlabel('Smokers')
plt.ylabel("Charges in ksh")
plt.title('Smoker Status Impact on Charges')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

Key Insights¶

The boxplot shows the impact of smoking status on medical charges. Individuals who smoke (“yes”) have significantly higher medical charges compared to non-smokers (“no”). The median charge for smokers is around 19,000, while for non-smokers it is much lower, around 7,000. The spread of charges among smokers is also relatively high, indicating greater variability in healthcare costs. Non-smokers generally have lower charges, although there are a few outliers with unusually high expenses. Overall, the plot clearly suggests that smoking is strongly associated with increased medical charges, making smoking status an important factors influencing healthcare costs

In [73]:
sns.boxplot(x='sex', y='charges',palette="Set1", data=df7)
plt.xlabel('Sex')
plt.ylabel("Charges in ksh")
plt.title('Sex impact on charges')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()
In [74]:
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import seaborn as sns 
sns.boxplot(x='region', y='charges',palette="Set1", data=df7)
plt.xlabel('Region')
plt.ylabel("Charges in ksh")
plt.title('Region impact on Charges')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

Key Insights:¶

The chart shows that while medical charges are relatively similar across most regions, the northeast tends to be the most expensive, with a higher average (median) cost than the others. The southwest, southeast, and northwest all share a similar baseline cost of around 7,500, but the southeast and northeast see much wider price swings, with some charges climbing significantly higher. Essentially, you're most likely to pay more in the northeast, though every region has at least some instances of high-cost cases.

Count Plot¶

In [72]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))

sns.countplot(df7, x='sex',  hue='smoker', palette='Set1')

plt.title('Smoking Status by Sex')
plt.xlabel('Sex')
plt.ylabel('Count')

plt.show()

Comment:¶

The chart shows that most people in both groups (male and female) are non-smokers. Female non-smokers are slightly more than male non-smokers, while the number of smokers is low and almost equal for both sexes. Overall, smoking is much less common compared to non-smoking in this dataset.

Heatmap (Correlation Matrix)¶

In [73]:
corr_matrix = df7.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt='.2f', linewidths=0.5)
plt.title('Correlation Matrix')
plt.show()

Key Isights¶

The correlation heatmap shows the strength and direction of relationships among the variables age, BMI, children, and charges. Age has the strongest positive correlation with charges (0.52), indicating that charges tend to increase as age increases. Children also show a weak positive correlation with charges (0.11), meaning people with more children may have slightly higher charges. BMI has a very weak negative correlation with charges (-0.06), suggesting almost no linear relationship between BMI and charges in this dataset. The correlations among the independent variables themselves are also very low (age–BMI = 0.15, age–children = 0.03, BMI–children = -0.01), indicating that the variables are largely independent of each other and there is little risk of multicollinearity. Overall, the heatmap suggests that age is the variable most strongly associated with charges, while BMI and children have relatively weak relationships.

Hypothesis Testing¶

H0:There is no significant difference between the sample mean of age and the population mean of charges

H1:There is a significant difference between the sample mean (age) and population mean (charges)

In [74]:
import pingouin as pg             # Necessary library 
# Perform one-sample t-test against a population mean of 25000
age=df7['age']
test_value = 30
one_sample_test = pg.ttest(age, test_value)
one_sample_test
Out[74]:
T dof alternative p_val CI95 cohen_d power BF10
T_test 20.197791 1103 two-sided 1.939394e-77 [37.62, 39.26] 0.607882 1.0 5.438e+73

Key Insights¶

Since the p_value is < 0.05 we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that there is a significant difference between the sample mean (age) and population mean(charges).

Two sample t-test¶

In [75]:
import pingouin as pg             # necessary library 

# Separate age data by gender, dropping missing values
group_A = df7[df7['sex'] == 'male']['charges'].dropna()
group_B = df7[df7['sex'] == 'male']['charges'].dropna()

# Perform independent two-sample t-test (Welch's t-test)
two_sample_test = pg.ttest(group_A, group_B, paired=False)
two_sample_test
Out[75]:
T dof alternative p_val CI95 cohen_d power BF10
T_test 0.0 1076 two-sided 1.0 [-668.63, 668.63] 0.0 0.05 0.068

Conclusion¶

Since the p_value is > 0.05 we fail to reject the null hypothesis and conclude that there is no significant differance between sex and charges

Chi_Square Test¶

In [76]:
import pandas as pd
from scipy import stats

contingency_table = pd.crosstab(df7["sex"], df7["smoker"])

# perform chi-square test
chi2, p, dof, expected = stats.chi2_contingency(contingency_table)

print("Chi-Square Test Result (sex vs smoker):")
print("Chi2:", chi2, "p-value:", p, "Degrees of Freedom:", dof)
print("p-value:", p)
print("Degrees of Freedom:", dof)

# Interpret the result
alpha = 0.05
if p < alpha:
    print("Reject the null hypothesis: there is a significant association between the variables.")
else:
    print("Fail to reject the null hypothesis: no significant association between the variables.")

Chi-Square Test Result (sex vs smoker):
Chi2: 0.20627615226825655 p-value: 0.6497019073792574 Degrees of Freedom: 1
p-value: 0.6497019073792574
Degrees of Freedom: 1
Fail to reject the null hypothesis: no significant association between the variables.

Key Insights¶

Since the p_value is > 0.05 we fail to reject the null hypothesis and conclude that there is a significant association between sex and smoker

Modeling¶

Regresssion Analysis¶

In [77]:
import statsmodels.api as sm
x=df7[["age","bmi","children"]]
y=df7["charges"]
# Fill missing values with column mean
x = x.fillna(x.mean())


x = sm.add_constant(x)

# Run regression
model = sm.OLS(y, x).fit()

print(model.summary())

                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================
Dep. Variable:                charges   R-squared:                       0.296
Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.294
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     154.3
Date:                Tue, 19 May 2026   Prob (F-statistic):           1.89e-83
Time:                        22:51:06   Log-Likelihood:                -10868.
No. Observations:                1104   AIC:                         2.174e+04
Df Residuals:                    1100   BIC:                         2.176e+04
Df Model:                           3                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
==============================================================================
                 coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
const       3922.8467    780.985      5.023      0.000    2390.457    5455.236
age          209.7498     10.024     20.926      0.000     190.082     229.417
bmi         -127.9990     23.814     -5.375      0.000    -174.725     -81.273
children     416.5998    113.261      3.678      0.000     194.368     638.831
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                      418.629   Durbin-Watson:                   1.920
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):             1156.554
Skew:                           2.022   Prob(JB):                    7.20e-252
Kurtosis:                       5.965   Cond. No.                         286.
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.

Key Insights¶

About 29.6% of the variation in charges is explained by age, BMI, and children, while 70% is affected by other factors that are not in this model

For every 1-year increase in age, charges increase by about 210 units, holding other variables constant.

Since the p-value is very small(less than 0.05), age is a significant predictor.

For every 1-unit increase in BMI, charges decrease by about 128 units.

This result is statistically significant.

Each additional child increases charges by about 417 units, holding other variables constant.People with more children tend to have higher charges.
